tabConfigs.description: This dashboard has been deprecated. See the release notes. This is part of 2 dashboards that cover Cluster Performance. This focuses on contention experienced by VMs in vSphere clusters. VM contention is the primary counter for performance, while utilization is the secondary counter. It lists all the clusters with their contention metrics. Select a cluster, and its detail contention over time will be automatically shown.
tabConfigs.name: (DEP) Cluster Contention

tabConfigs.description: This dashboard has been deprecated. See the release notes. This is part of 2 dashboards that cover Cluster Performance. This focuses on  the utilization. Value is not rolled up as the context here is performance and not capacity. Utilization complements contention in performance management. It lists all the  clusters with their utilization. Select a cluster, and its detail utilization over time will be automatically shown. For vSAN cluster, use this in conjunction with the vSAN Cluster performance dashboards, as these focus on non vSAN parts.
tabConfigs.name: (DEP) Cluster Utilization

tabConfigs.description: This dashboard has been deprecated. See the release notes. This is part of a set of 8 dashboards (4 VM + 4 Infrastructure) that covers configuration that do not follow best practices. Use them together so you see the big picture. This dashboard focuses on incorrect VM configuration. It lists VM whose configuration need to be fixed. No data means the configuration is correct. More details on the dashboard is on the Read Me widget.
tabConfigs.name: (DEP) Consumer \ Correct it?

tabConfigs.description: This dashboard has been deprecated. See the release notes. This is part of a set of 8 dashboards (4 VM + 4 Infrastructure) that covers configuration that do not follow best practices. Use them together so you see the big picture. This dashboard focuses on suboptimal VM configuration. It lists VM whose configuration increase your cost. Keep them minimal. No data means the costly configuration do not exist. More details on the dashboard is on the Read Me widget.
tabConfigs.name: (DEP) Consumer \ Optimize it?

tabConfigs.description: This dashboard has been deprecated. See the release notes. This is part of a set of 8 dashboards (4 VM + 4 Infrastructure) that covers configuration that do not follow best practices. Use them together so you see the big picture. This dashboard focuses on complex VM configuration. It lists VM whose configuration increase your operations complexity. Keep them minimal. No data means the complex configuration do not exist. More details on the dashboard is on the Read Me widget.
tabConfigs.name: (DEP) Consumer \ Simplify It?

tabConfigs.description: This dashboard has been deprecated. See the release notes. This is part of a set of 8 dashboards (4 VM + 4 Infrastructure) that covers configuration that do not follow best practices. Use them together so you see the big picture. This dashboard focuses on outdated VM configuration. It lists VM whose configuration need to be updated, ideally to the latest release. No data means the configuration is correct. More details on the dashboard is on the Read Me widget.
tabConfigs.name: (DEP) Consumer \ Update it?

tabConfigs.name: (DEP) VM Contention

tabConfigs.description: This dashboard has been deprecated. See the release notes. This is part of 2 dashboards that cover VM Performance. This focuses on the utilization. Value is not rolled up as the context here is performance and not capacity. Utilization complements contention in performance management. It lists all the VMs with their utilization. Select a VM, and its detail utilization over time will be automatically shown.
tabConfigs.name: (DEP) VM Utilization

tabConfigs.description: Single-dashboard capacity review for a VCF Ops admin. Top section shows environment-wide capacity headroom at cluster granularity with post-HA adjustments, days-until-runout projections from VCF Ops capacity analytics, and 'how many more VMs fit' counts. Bottom section identifies right-sizing opportunities — VMs VCF Ops's capacity engine has flagged as oversized, undersized, or idle, with 95th-percentile demand justification columns and explicit current-to-target resize recommendations. Colors follow the standard three-band stoplight throughout. Use for quarterly capacity reviews and before any cluster-scale VM deployment.
tabConfigs.name: [VCF Content Factory] Capacity Assessment & Right-Sizing

tabConfigs.description: Demand-side counterpart to [VCF Content Factory] Capacity Assessment & Right-Sizing. Where that dashboard answers "how much headroom is left (allocation basis)?", this one answers "where is contention happening right now, and which clusters, hosts, and VMs are feeling it?". All metrics are demand- and contention-derived: CPU Ready, co-stop, balloon, swap, vSAN latency — not vCPU/vRAM ratios. Use alongside the allocation dashboard for a complete pre- and post-deployment capacity picture. Pick a Datacenter or vSphere World from the left, drill into a cluster row to surface the VMs and hosts driving demand pressure on that cluster.
tabConfigs.name: [VCF Content Factory] Demand-Driven Capacity Planning

tabConfigs.description: Demand-driven capacity planning v2. Combines percentage-first cluster metrics, usable-vs-provisioned CPU comparison, contention heatmaps for visual scanning, and detailed host outlier + vSAN contribution tables from v1. Narrow scope picker (w=2) with wide cluster overview beside it. Select a cluster row to drill into VM contention, host outliers, and per-host vSAN performance. Two interaction tiers: broad visual scanning (heatmaps) at DC level, then drill-down detail (tables) at cluster level.
tabConfigs.nameOriginal: [VCF Content Factory] Demand-Driven Capacity Planning v2
tabConfigs.name: [VCF Content Factory] Demand-Driven Capacity Planning v2

tabConfigs.description: VP-level environment configuration and capacity status dashboard. Three sections: capacity headroom overview with post-HA adjusted KPIs and cluster-level detail; ESXi patch and version consistency across the host fleet; hardware and BIOS firmware configuration inventory. Designed for quarterly reviews and executive reporting. All data is self-provided (no picker required) — shows the entire vSphere environment.
tabConfigs.name: [VCF Content Factory] Environment Configuration Status

tabConfigs.description: Per-vCenter breakdown of VM vCPU by role (VKS Worker, Supervisor CP, vCLS, VM Service, Regular) plus physical cores, vCPU:pCore ratio, and the computed VKS core equivalent. One row per vCenter — no scoping widget, this is a read-at-a-glance report. Replaces the per-vCenter PowerCLI VKS core- consumption script.
tabConfigs.name: [VCF Content Factory] VKS Core Consumption

tabConfigs.description: Pick a vCenter from the list on the left to see its virtual machines with key CPU and memory performance metrics. Demonstrates the ResourceList -> View widget interaction (resourceId) pattern.
tabConfigs.nameOriginal: [VCF Content Factory] VM Performance by vCenter
tabConfigs.name: [VCF Content Factory] VM Performance by vCenter

tabConfigs.description: Single-screen demo dashboard for VM rightsizing analysis across the entire environment. Opens to a sortable VM list with 30-day 95th-percentile CPU and memory demand columns alongside configured sizing. Sort any column to surface oversized (low demand, high configured), undersized (high demand + ready time), or idle VM candidates without any filter or picker interaction.
tabConfigs.name: [VCF Content Factory] VM Rightsizing Demo

tabConfigs.description: vSAN-focused companion to [VCF Content Factory] Demand-Driven Capacity Planning. vSAN cluster metrics live on VirtualSANDCCluster (VirtualAndPhysicalSANAdapter), a parallel object hierarchy to ClusterComputeResource, so cross-kind filtering from the main dashboard cannot surface them. Pick a vSAN cluster from the left to populate the performance and health/resync detail views.
tabConfigs.name: [VCF Content Factory] vSAN Cluster Health

tabConfigs.name: vSphere Compute Performance

tabConfigs.description:
Proactive health check. Done by someone familiar with the environment to gain insight to prevent alert.
Ideally, you do this twice per day, as the workload pattern could vary throughout the day. 
Review once at the start of the day to prevent alerts during the day and once again at the end of the day to prevent alerts overnight.
tabConfigs.name: vSphere Daily Check

tabConfigs.description:
Part of Operationalize Your World.

The dashboard to monitor Windows and/or Linux. It focuses on performance.
tabConfigs.name: Windows OS discovered by Telegraf

tabConfigs.description:
This dashboard provides a quick configuration summary of all the key objects associated with workload management such as Supervisor Clusters, Namespaces, Pods, and Tanzu Kubernetes clusters.

It is essential that the configuration is consistent across all the objects. Configuration drifts may result in inconsistent performance or availability of the applications leveraging workload management kubernetes constructs.
tabConfigs.name: Workload Management Configuration

tabConfigs.description:
The Workload Management Inventory dashboards curate the Kubernetes inventory across all the Workload Management-enabled vSphere environments into a single pane of glass. 


This includes an end to end topology map showcasing the health of all the objects along with upstream and downstream dependencies.

Upon clicking any object in the relationship tree, the related inventory of Supervisor Clusters, Namespaces, Pods, Developer Managed VMs, and Tanzu Kubernetes clusters can be viewed and exported from this dashboard.
tabConfigs.name: Workload Management Inventory
